Timing Matters: A Comprehensive Meta-Analysis on the Optimal Period for Cranioplasty After Severe Traumatic Brain Injury
Lucca B. Palavani, MS *, M´ arcio Yuri Ferreira, MD ‡ , Raphael Camerotte, MS § , Lucas Pari Mitre, MS || , Lucas Piason, MD ¶ , Filipi Fim Andreão, MS § , Anna Luiza Pereira, MS # , Ruy Monteiro, MD**, Robson Luis Oliveira de Amorim, MD, PhD ‡‡ , Raphael Bertani, MD §§ , Wellingson Paiva, MD, PhD ||||

*Faculty of Medicine, Max Planck University Center, Indaiatuba, São Paulo, Brazil; ‡ Department of Neurosurgery, Lenox Hill, New York, New York, USA; § Faculty of Medicine, Federal University of Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil; || Faculty of Medicine, Santa Casa de São Paulo School of Medical Sciences, São Paulo, Brazil; ¶ Faculty of Medicine, Bahiana School of Medicine and Public Health, Salvador, Brazil; # Faculty of Medicine, Rio de Janeiro State University, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil;

of Medicine, Rio de Janeiro State University, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil; **Neurological Surgery Service, Hospital Municipal Miguel Couto, R´ ı o de Janeiro, Brazil; ‡‡ Health Sciences Postgraduation Program, Federal University of Amazonas, Amazonas, Brazil; §§ Department of Neurosurgery, São Paulo University, São Paulo, São Paulo, Brazil; |||| Experimental Surgery Laboratory and Division of Neurological Surgery, University of São Paulo Medical School, São Paulo, Brazil

Correspondence: Anna Luiza Pereira, MS, Faculty of Medicine, Rio de Janeiro State University, Blvd. 28 de Setembro, 77 Vila Isabel, Rio de Janeiro, RJ 20.551-030, Brazil. Email: annapereira2004@gmail.com
Received, May 10, 2024; Accepted, August 20, 2024; Published Online, November 13, 2024.
© Congress of Neurological Surgeons 2024. All rights reserved.

BACKGROUND AND OBJECTIVES: Craniectomy is a commonly performed procedure in severe traumatic brain injury (TBI) to control intracranial hypertension and reduce mortality. The optimal timing for cranioplasty after craniectomy remains a topic of debate. The aim of this study was to investigate the ideal timing for cranioplasty after severe TBI, focusing on complications associated with temporal aspects and materials used in cranioplasty.

METHODS: Multiple databases, including PubMed, Embase, Cochrane, and Web of Science, were searched for studies reporting on the subject. Inclusion criteria involved randomized and observational studies comparing early and late cranioplasty procedures. Quality assessment was performed using the Methodological Index for Non-Randomized Studies scale. Results were pooled in a single-arm meta-analysis and presented as mean and 95% con /uniFB01 dence interval. RESULTS: Early cranioplasty was

mean and 95% con /uniFB01 dence interval. RESULTS: Early cranioplasty was associated with a lower likelihood of subdural effusion (odds ratio [OR] 0.3735 [0.1643; 0.8490], P =.0187). However, no differences were detected for the presence of infection between both groups (OR 0.7460 [0.2065; 2.6945], P = .6548). Cranioplasties performed within or equal to 3 months from the TBI incident were associated with a reduced likelihood of minor complications (OR 0.4471 [0.2467; 0.8102], P = .0080).

likelihood of minor complications (OR 0.4471 [0.2467; 0.8102], P = .0080). Paradoxically, this time frame exhibited an elevated risk of hydrocephalus (OR 3.2035 [1.4860; 6.9059], P = .0030), as well as total complications (OR 1.4190 [1.0453; 1.9262] P = .0248).

CONCLUSION: This comprehensive review highlights the current lack of consensus on the optimal timing for cranioplasty after severe TBI. Early cranioplasty, within 35 days, showed a reduced risk of subdural effusion, but no disparity in the odds of infection was found. A reduced incidence of minor complications was observed in the earlier approach, however with an elevated risk for hydrocephalus and total complications. The association between timing and complications, including hydrocephalus,
The association between timing and complications, including hydrocephalus, highlights the need for further research and standardization in this critical aspect of TBI management.

KEY WORDS: Cranioplasty, Traumatic brain injury, Complications, Humans, Meta-analysis, Treatment outcome
C raniectomy (DC) is one of the most commonly performed procedures in the setting of severe traumatic brain injury (TBI) to control intracranial hypertension and reduce mortality in these patients. This situation generates a signi /uniFB01 cant number of patients requiring cranioplasty. Cranioplasty (CP) is a

ABBREVIATIONS: CP, cranioplasty; DC, craniectomy; SSFS, sinking skin /uniFB02 ap syndrome; TBI, traumatic brain injury.
prominent method for addressing skull deformities for cosmetic, protection, and, in some cases, functional improvement purposes. 1-3 The therapeutic ef /uniFB01 cacy is underscored by evident improvements in neuropsychological and cognitive functions after CP and notable enhancements in cerebral blood /uniFB02 ow and cerebrospinal /uniFB02 uid circulation. 4-7

Although statistically signi /uniFB01 cant associations with better outcomes have been observed in patients undergoing early CP, 8 the ideal timing for CP remains a subject of contention in the existing
literature, as highlighted by varying perspectives. 9,10 Notably, some studies have reported that the timing of CP is not correlated with certain complications, such as the risk of infection. 11,12

Early procedures, within 3 months, were associated with improved neurological function outcomes. 13 Conversely, alternative studies support a delayed CP approach, suggesting intervention within 6 months after DC. 14,15 This delayed timing is justi /uniFB01 ed by observations of good functional recovery and a reduced risk of complications. The absence of a standardized time frame underscores the ongoing debate and divergence in recommendations across various research /uniFB01 ndings.

To investigate the optimal timing for CP after severe TBI, we conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of the current literature. We focused on complications and outcomes related to the timing and materials used in CP, aiming to provide a comprehensive understanding of the best practices for this procedure.

This systematic review and meta-analysis was performed following the handbook of the Cochrane Collaboration and the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis statement guidelines.

Inclusion in this meta-analysis was restricted to studies that met all the following criteria: (1) randomized and observational studies, (2) reporting on intraprocedural or follow-up complications of CP after DC due to TBI, (3) comparing early and late CP procedures, and (4) studies reporting 4 or more patients. We excluded non-English articles, reviews, letters to the editor, abstracts, and commentaries from the initial assessment.

We searched for complications after CP post-DC due to TBI across multiple databases, including PubMed, Embase, Cochrane, and Web of Science. The search strategy used terms related to ' cranioplasty ' or to ' cranial defect, ' ' calvarial defect ' and ' skull defect, ' such as ' skull repair, ' ' bone /uniFB02 ap replacement ' and ' cranial replacement, ' coupled with keywords such as ' traumatic brain injury, ' ' TBI, ' ' decompressive craniectomy, ' and ' DC. ' A prospective registration was
' decompressive craniectomy, ' and ' DC. ' A prospective registration was not performed for this study.

Search results were imported into Rayyan QCRI software for manual duplicate removal and initial screening based on titles and abstracts. This stage was conducted by 2 independent reviewers (L.B.P. and L.M.). Disagreements were resolved by the senior author (W.P.). The studies selected in this preliminary phase underwent full-text screening by 2 independent reviewers (L.M. and M.Y.F.). Con /uniFB02 icts were resolved by a third independent reviewer (L.B.P.).

The time between TBI and CP was divided as follows for the singlearmed and two-armed analysis: ' early ' was considered for ≤ 35 days of surgical reconstruction and ' late ' for procedures happening after >35 days from TBI incident. Cranioplasties occurring after 3 months of TBI incident were considered as ' ultra-late. ' Owing to the signi /uniFB01 cant variation among studies in how they de /uniFB01 ne a ' complication, ' we established our de /uniFB01 nition as any postoperative situation

' we established our de /uniFB01 nition as any postoperative situation necessitating further intervention. Major and minor complications were classi /uniFB01 ed according to the following criteria: major complications were de /uniFB01 ned as being potentially life-threatening or if they brought permanent de /uniFB01 cits to the patient, whereas minor complications were de /uniFB01 ned as not potentially life-threatening or not bringing permanent de /uniFB01 cits to the patients. Data were

or not bringing permanent de /uniFB01 cits to the patients. Data were sought regarding participant age and sex, publication date, number of patients receiving early CP, patients receiving late or ultra-late CP, overall complications, subdural /uniFB02 uid collection, infection rate, CP material, the severity of TBI, type of TBI/mechanism of injury, and mean operative time.

We assessed study quality using the Methodological Index for NonRandomized Studies scale. This scale has 8 items for noncomparative studies and 4 for comparative studies. As our meta-analysis included only comparative studies, we used the full 12-item Methodological Index for Non-Randomized Studies scale. This allowed us to evaluate the methodological rigor and quality of the studies included in our analysis.

A comparative analysis with odds ratio (OR) and 95% CIs was used, and P value <.05 was considered signi /uniFB01 cant. A random effects model was adopted. I 2 statistics were used to assess for heterogeneity; I 2 > 35% was considered signi /uniFB01 cant. Statistical analysis was performed using R Studio software (version 4.2.3, R Foundation for Statistical Computing). The Mantel-Haenszel method, the IV method, and the Restricted Maximum Likelihood were used.

A total of 3407 articles were identi /uniFB01 ed through a search of multiple databases: 1028 in PubMed, 1685 in Embase, 653 in Web of Science, and 41 in Cochrane. Of these, 1112 nonduplicate citations were screened, excluding 981 articles because of their lack of relevance based on title and abstract review. The remaining 131 articles underwent full-text review, of which 118 were excluded because of the lack of description of procedurerelated complications, the absence of TBI in the patients,

of procedurerelated complications, the absence of TBI in the patients, or the inability to compare 2 different time intervals between DC and CP. Finally, 13 studies met the criteria for inclusion in this review and meta-analysis. One article was excluded after data extraction because it did not meet the required criteria. Thus, 12 studies were ultimately analyzed, and the details of the study selection process are illustrated in Figure 1.

The quality assessment of all 12 included studies is demonstrated below in Table 1.
FIGURE 1. Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and MetaAnalysis /uniFB02 ow diagram. This /uniFB01 gure is original to this submission, so no credit or license is needed.

The analysis used data from 12 studies involving 1371 patients with TBI treated with CP. Each study had a patient range from 30 to 435. In adult-only cohort studies (n = 8, 66%), the mean patient age fell between the 4th and 6th decades of life. Operative times were reported in 33% of studies (n = 4), ranging from 59 to 180 minutes per surgery. TBI mechanisms were categorized based on various factors, including skull penetration, intracranial pressure, bleeding, and parenchymal complications,

intracranial pressure, bleeding, and parenchymal complications, in 25% of studies (n = 3). TBI severity was assessed differently across studies, with 1 study classifying TBIs as ' severe, ' 1 using the Extended Glasgow Outcome Scale, and the remaining 3 stratifying based on the Glasgow Coma Scale. All studies recorded cranioplasties as either ' early ' or ' late, ' with Eaton (2022) including ' Ultra early ' and ' intermediary ' time cutoffs. The most common time cutoff for considering CP as '

' time cutoffs. The most common time cutoff for considering CP as ' late ' was 90 days (n = 5), with the earliest being 30 days (n = 1). Detailed patient baseline characteristics from the included studies are provided in Table 2.

Twelve studies were included in this meta-analysis, covering 1371 patients with TBI undergoing CP. Two studies involved both adult and pediatric populations, while the rest focused solely on adult patients (Table 2). Autologous bone tissue was the most commonly used CP material (66% of studies), followed by titanium mesh (25% of studies), and methyl methacrylate cement in 1 study (8% of studies). Among major complications, the most frequently reported was postoperative subdural /uniFB02 uid

the most frequently reported was postoperative subdural /uniFB02 uid collection, affecting 132 patients (9% of patients), followed by hematoma in 81 (6% of patients), hydrocephalus in 65 (4% of patients), bone resorption in 38 (2% of patients), sinking skin /uniFB02 ap syndrome (SSFS) in 31 (2% of patients), seizures in 28 (2% of patients), infection in 17 (1% of patients), wound healing complications in 9 (0.6% of patients), and /uniFB02 uid accumulation below the skin /uniFB02 ap in 4 (0.2%

and /uniFB02 uid accumulation below the skin /uniFB02 ap in 4 (0.2% of patients). Minor complications were described in 7 studies (58% of studies). The most frequently reported minor complication, although from 1 study only (Yang et al), was ventriculomegaly in 66 patients (38% of all minor complications), followed by 38 cases of bone resorption, 7 wound healing disturbances, 5 soft tissue injuries, 2 cerebrospinal /uniFB02 uid leaks, and 2 wound dehiscence. In an aggregate metric, considering

uid leaks, and 2 wound dehiscence. In an aggregate metric, considering multiple major and minor complications experienced by individual patients, 577 patient-level complications were reported (42% of patients), comprising 405 major (29.5% of patients) and 172 minor (12.5% of patients). Detailed major and minor complications per study are available in Table 3.

Studies used different time cutoffs for de /uniFB01 ning ' early ' and ' late ' CP, with Eaton et al, 36 , Tora et al, 3 , and Zhang et al 35 using the longest cutoffs (over 90 days). Eaton et al 36 further divided this into ' ultra-early ' and ' ultra-late ' cohorts and outlined an institutional readiness assessment protocol. Comparing ' early ' and ' late ' cohorts, patients undergoing early CP had fewer subdural effusions (OR 0.3735 [0.1643; 0.8490], P = .0187, Figure 2), with no difference
(OR 0.3735 [0.1643; 0.8490], P = .0187, Figure 2), with no difference in infection rates (OR 0.7460 [0.2065; 2.6945], P = .6548, Figure 3).

Patients with < = 45 days post-TBI were analyzed separately, showing the following complication rates: /uniFB02 uid accumulation below skin /uniFB02 ap: 0% ([0.00; 0.02]), hematoma: 3% ([0.00; 0.06], I 2 = 83%), hydrocephalus: 2% ([0.00; 0.04]), infection: 4% ([0.01; 0.07], I 2 = 68%), seizure: 2% ([0.00; 0.04]), and subdural effusion: 2% ([0.00; 0.04], I 2 = 84%). SSFS diagnosis ranged around 3% ([0.00; 0.06], I 2 = 83%). Aggregate complication rates were as follows: total complications: 26%

Aggregate complication rates were as follows: total complications: 26% ([0.20; 0.32], I 2 = 96%), major complications: 24% ([0.20; 0.28], I 2 = 99%), and minor complications: 1% ([0.00; 0.03], I 2 = 78%).

The same analysis was conducted for all CP patients until the ' ultra-late ' cutoff (up to 3 months post-TBI), revealing the following proportions: /uniFB02 uid accumulation below skin /uniFB02 ap: 0% ([0.00; 0.01]), hematoma presence: 2% ([0.01; 0.03], I 2 = 66%), hydrocephalus presence: 2% ([0.00; 0.03]), infection presence:

4% ([0.02; 0.05], I 2 = 59%), seizure occurrence: 1% ([0.00; 0.02]), and subdural effusion presence: 0% ([0.00; 0.01], I 2 = 65%). SSFS diagnosis ranged around 0% ([0.00; 0.01]). Aggregate complication rates were 19% for total complications ([0.16; 0.22], I 2 = 93%), 17% for major complications ([0.15; 0.20], I 2 = 98%), and 0% for minor complications ([0.00; 0.01], I 2 = 73%).

The patient cohort was divided into subgroups based on the type of graft used: autologous and nonautologous. For autologous grafts, /uniFB02 uid accumulation below the skin /uniFB02 ap was 0% ([0.00; 0.02]), the presence of hematoma was 1% ([0.00; 0.03]), and the presence of hydrocephalus was 0% ([0.02; 0.02]). The presence of infection was 3% ([0.00; 0.06], I 2 = 59%; Figure 4). The occurrence of seizure was 0% ([0.00; 0.01]), and the presence of subdural effusion was 0% ([0.00; 0.02], I 2 =

0.01]), and the presence of subdural effusion was 0% ([0.00; 0.02], I 2 = 0%; Figure 5). The development of SSFS was 0% ([0.00; 0.01]). Total complications were 17% ([0.12; 0.21], I 2 = 93%; Figure 6), with minor complications at 2% ([0.00; 0.05], I 2 = 88%; Figure 7) and major complications at 11% ([0.07; 0.16], I 2 = 74%; Figure 8). Bone resorption was observed at 2% ([0.00; 0.04], I 2 = 75%).

For nonautologous grafts, /uniFB02 uid accumulation below the skin /uniFB02 ap was 1% ([0.00; 0.04]). The presence of hematoma was 0% ([0.00; 0.03] -no cases), and the presence of hydrocephalus was 0% ([0.00; 0.03] -no cases). The presence of infection was 0% ([0.00; 0.03] -no cases; Figure 9). The occurrence of seizure was 1% ([0.00; 0.04]), and the presence of subdural effusion was 3%

([0.00; 0.07], I 2 = 22%; Figure 10). The development of SSFS was 0% ([0.00; 0.03] -no cases). Total complications were 8% ([0.02; 0.14], I 2 = 0%; Figure 11), with minor complications at 3% ([0.00; 0.07], I 2 = 29%; Figure 12) and major complications at 2% ([0.00; 0.05], I 2 = 13%; Figure 13). More detailed information on for parameters obtained for each subgroup on Table 4:

Finally, all CP cohorts were compared with the 3-month ' ultralate ' cutoff. Cranioplasties performed within 3 months of the TBI incident were associated with an increased risk of total complications (OR 1.4, P = .02, Figure 14). Paradoxically, they were linked to fewer minor complications (OR 0.4, P = .008, Figure 15). No differences were observed in major complications (Figure 16), infection rate, subdural effusion, presence of seizures, or hematoma, compared with the ultra-late cohort. In

presence of seizures, or hematoma, compared with the ultra-late cohort. In addition, cranioplasties within 3 months were associated with an increased risk of hydrocephalus (OR 3.2, P = .003, Figure 17).

This review incorporates 12 studies, involving 1371 adult and pediatric patients with TBI undergoing CP. However, limited high-quality evidence addresses key clinical questions. CP, using either a preserved autologous bone /uniFB02 ap or synthetic prosthesis, can restore a degree of mechanical protection to the brain. It aims to

diminish the risk of falls by enhancing vestibular system equilibrium, addressing the syndrome of trephined, and simultaneously improving craniofacial aesthetics and neurological performance, particularly regarding the magnitude of change in early indications postcraniectomy. 8,16-18

In general, reported complication rates from CP, regardless of timing, range from 15% to 30%, 22 and a comprehensive systematic review revealed a pooled overall rate of 19.5% for all CP indications and a rate of 25.6% for TBI indications. 21 In our
FIGURE 12. <90 days single-arm meta-analysis of minor complications in patients receiving nonautologous cranioplasty. This /uniFB01 gure is original to this submission, so no credit or license is needed.

analysis, the total complication rate was 26.9%, and considering multiple complications experienced by individual patients, 1179 of 1371 patient-level complications were reported, representing 86% of the patients. Frequently encountered complications encompass infection, intracranial hemorrhage, extra-axial /uniFB02 uid collections, hydrocephalus, seizures, and bone resorption.

The optimal timing for CP remains highly variable. 25 Traditionally, it has been suggested that performing CP around 6 months postinjury minimizes the risk of infection 26,27 or complications 20 after DC. By contrast, Oh et al 28 observed signi /uniFB01 cantly higher infection rates in the late CP group. Recent research challenges the idea that a 6-month delay is necessary to minimize the infection risk, and these studies show no statistical difference in infection rates and most complications

show no statistical difference in infection rates and most complications between different timing intervals besides an increase in odds of hydrocephalus with early CP. 21-23 The timing varied widely between the TBI indication series, with different cutoffs ranging from 30 days, 29-31 35 days, 32 2 months, 9,33,34 and 3 months 3,19,35 as relative for ultra-early and early descriptions. Only Eaton et al 36 followed the proposed time frame (postcraniotomy) cutoffs by the consensus meeting on

proposed time frame (postcraniotomy) cutoffs by the consensus meeting on posttraumatic CP 25 : ultra-early CP is up to 6 weeks after craniectomy, early CP is 6 weeks to 3 months, intermediate 3 to 6 months, and delayed more than 6 months. Although they recognize that clinical status should be considered when deciding the timing of CP, it can be helpful for clinical benchmarking and research purposes. 25

Our ultra-early analysis revealed a signi /uniFB01 cant decrease in the odds of subdural effusion with an earlier approach, while no difference in the odds of infection was observed. These /uniFB01 ndings
FIGURE 14. <90 days vs >90 days cutoff of cranioplasty for total complications. This /uniFB01 gure is original to this submission, so no credit or license is needed.

FIGURE 15. <90 days vs >90 days cutoff of cranioplasty for minor complications. This /uniFB01 gure is original to this submission, so no credit or license is needed.
are consistent with previous results, although previous studies combined reports with longer time frame cutoffs, 21,24 suggesting that even earlier approaches have little impact on infection rates but may offer bene /uniFB01 ts in reducing /uniFB02 uid collection.

We de /uniFB01 ned cutoff timing as 35 days, relying on the cutoff established by Yang et al, 32 which carried signi /uniFB01 cant weight in our analysis. Patient-level data were available from Chun and colleagues, 30 revealing that none in the control group underwent CP in fewer than 35 days. In their cohort, ultra-early surgery facilitated intraoperative dissection plane de /uniFB01 nition without additional complications, such as infection, subdural hygroma, or brain parenchymal hemorrhage,

such as infection, subdural hygroma, or brain parenchymal hemorrhage, as observed in the control group. The only limitation arises from Morgan et al, 29 who did not specify the exact date for the control group undergoing late CP. However, the mean timing for their 6 control patients was 86.83 days (SD = 44.82), minimizing the risk of erroneously categorizing any of these patients within the 30 to 35-day interval.

In our assessment comparing early with intermediate and delayed CP (within 3 months vs more than 3 months), we observed a higher association with hydrocephalus in the earlier approach, with no differences in infection rate, seizures, or hematoma, consistent with previous studies. 21,24,37 However, with an expanded study pool, no difference was noted in the odds of subdural effusion, contradicting previous systematic reviews. 21 Furthermore, the earlier approach was linked to fewer minor

reviews. 21 Furthermore, the earlier approach was linked to fewer minor complications, with no discernible differences in major complications, but posed an elevated risk of total complications. Similarly, Schuss et al 9 related that the early group suffered signi /uniFB01 cantly more complications (25.9%) compared with the delayed group (14.2%) in their study, besides there was no description of speci /uniFB01 c complications.

Autologous bone is frequently favored over allograft devices due to its biological characteristics, optimal compatibility with the
FIGURE 17. <90 days vs >90 days cutoff of cranioplasty for hydrocephalus. This /uniFB01 gure is original to this submission, so no credit or license is needed.

bony defect, and lower cost. 38 By contrast, allograft has traditionally been considered a secondary option because of perceived higher risks of infection and rejection and associated costs, 39 despite recent materials showing potential for superior cosmetic results. 40,41 Notably, a previous meta-analysis found no signi /uniFB01 -cant difference in infection rates. 32

The literature links few adverse events directly to these materials, besides bone /uniFB02 ap resorption which is a complication speci /uniFB01 cally associated with autologous CP. 42-46 In adults, the reported incidence of bone /uniFB02 ap resorption ranges from 2% to 32%, 42,43 and in pediatric patients, the risk is higher, with rates increasing to 50% to 66%. 44,45 Our systematic review, however, reported a lower pooled incidence of bone /uniFB02 ap resorption at 4%. Notably, only 2 studies

incidence of bone /uniFB02 ap resorption at 4%. Notably, only 2 studies with pediatric populations reported this outcome: 17.2% in Piedra et al 10 and 16.7% in Morgan et al, 29 both irrespective of timing.

Our study has limitations worth noting. First, the lack of randomized studies is a constraint, as most studies are retrospective and single-center. In addition, signi /uniFB01 cant heterogeneity among the included studies introduces biases because of differences in methodologies, patient populations, and timing de /uniFB01 nitions, potentially leading to varied outcomes. Moreover, there is a shortage of studies involving pediatric populations, with limited subgroup analysis when present in

pediatric populations, with limited subgroup analysis when present in mixed studies, emphasizing the need for more research in this demographic for comprehensive conclusions. Studies ' differences in timing categorization of cranioplasties in different cohorts limited the number of possible de /uniFB01 nitions for our analysis; however, the 30-35 days window seemed to divide the timing for analysis functionally because this interval was not tangential but a single study. In addition, the

this interval was not tangential but a single study. In addition, the functional bene /uniFB01 ts of CP timing were not speci /uniFB01 cally assessed, representing a gap in our exploration. Last, the classi /uniFB01 cation between major and minor complications was not made entirely clear by the included studies. So, there is an interpretative component that in some cases may over or underestimate the results.

Despite the established signi /uniFB01 cance of CP, the review reveals a lack of high-quality evidence addressing key clinical questions, such as optimal timing, choice of material, and long-term outcomes. Complications after CP, regardless of timing, remain a signi /uniFB01 cant concern, with infection, intracranial hemorrhage, and bone resorption among the frequently encountered issues, and our analysis evidenced that a single patient may frequently experience multiple of those complications

a single patient may frequently experience multiple of those complications during the treatment course. The optimal timing for CP remains variable mainly due to the heterogeneity in the studies. This variability challenges the traditional notion of a 6-month delay to minimize the infection risk. Some studies suggest that earlier CP cohorts may have a lower risk of /uniFB02 uid recollection, while infection rates are comparable between early and late CP cohorts. Although current literature has

between early and late CP cohorts. Although current literature has followed a trend to favor earlier cranioplasties, we have not found evidence to support the claim. The intrinsic bias in the observational studies, especially regarding the de /uniFB01 nition of early and late cranioplasties, may have played a signi /uniFB01 cant role in this matter. Furthermore, the comparison between autograft and allograft materials highlights the complexities involved in material selection, with autologous
the complexities involved in material selection, with autologous bone tissue favored for its biological characteristics despite the risk of bone /uniFB02 ap resorption.
